message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Recently, [8] has proposed that heterogeneity of infectiousness (and susceptibility) across individuals in infectious diseases, plays a major role in affecting the Herd Immunity Thresh-old (HIT). Such heterogeneity has been observed in COVID-19 and is recognized as
                  <jats:italic>overdis-persion</jats:italic>
                  (or “super-spreading”). The model of [8] suggests that super-spreaders contribute significantly to the effective reproduction factor,
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  , and that they are likely to get infected and immune early in the process. Consequently, under
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  ≈ 3 (attributed to COVID-19), the Herd Immunity Threshold (HIT) is as low as 5%, in contrast to 67% according to the traditional models [1, 2, 4, 10].
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  This work follows up on [8] and proposes that heterogeneity of infectiousness (susceptibility) has two “faces” whose mix affects dramatically the HIT: (1)
                  <jats:italic>Personal-Trait-</jats:italic>
                  , and (2)
                  <jats:italic>Event-Based-</jats:italic>
                  Infectiousness (Susceptibility). The former is a personal trait of specific individuals (
                  <jats:italic>super-spreaders</jats:italic>
                  ) and is nullified once those individuals are immune (as in [8]). The latter is event-based (e.g cultural
                  <jats:italic>super-spreading</jats:italic>
                  events) and remains effective throughout the process, even after the super-spreaders immune. We extend [8]’s model to account for these two factors, analyze it and conclude that the HIT is very sensitive to the mix between (1) and (2), and under
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  ≈ 3 it can vary between 5% and 67%. Preliminary data from COVID-19 suggests that herd immunity is not reached at 5%.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  We address operational aspects and analyze the effects of lockdown strategies on the spread of a disease. We find that herd immunity (and HIT) is very sensitive to the lock-down type. While some lockdowns affect positively the disease blocking and increase herd immunity, others have
                  <jats:italic>adverse</jats:italic>
                  effects and
                  <jats:italic>reduce</jats:italic>
                  the herd immunity.
                </jats:p>
title: Super-Spreaders Out, Super-Spreading In: The Effects of Infectiousness Heterogeneity and Lockdowns on Herd Immunity
